text: This Review forms part of the overall review on cross-cultural strategies for business development based on research findings emanating from the United States of America (USA) and diverse African contexts. The entire review examines in details the culture, inequalities and innovations in the USA and various African countries and drawing comparative lessons. The current review highlights the global nature of business development that demands more than a surface-level awareness of cross-cultural challenges. While drawing from the USA business development experience and comparable trends in various African countries, this review sheds light on complex aspects of cross-cultural business development. Specifically, this review looks at the culture, market and innovations and derived business development strategies in the USA and Africa. It draws examples of cultural structures underlying businesses in the USA and Africa, while giving a peep into the inner workings of Silicon Valley and Wall Street highlighting unique cultural factors that are part and parcel of business development networks. In contrast, businesses in Africa must contend with the complexities of many countries each with its unique culture, business environment and several other issues such as market diversities and many inequalities which are addressed through varied strategies. On one hand, this review therefore highlights the USA business development networks, culture and its success at the innovation centres and professional networks; while on the other hand, it describes in detail the different strategies that have been implemented in Africa so as to cope with the culture and exploit the opportunities available in African emerging markets while looking into the many economic inequalities and challenges that affect businesses in Africa and therefore need adaptation of specific strategies so as to assure the success of business development in cross-cultural environment, which is also a prerequisite for planning for a better future for businesses and thus recommended for readers who are interested in cross-cultural business development issues and solutions in the USA and Africa.
